coredata.dc:title: Adenosine-derived doped carbon dots: From an insight into effect of N/P co-doping on emission to highly sensitive picric acid sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Na
Liu, Shi Gang
Fan, Yu Zhu
Ju, Yan Jun
Xiao, Na
Luo, Hong Qun
Li, Nian Bing
coredata.dc:description: The various synthetic routes of carbon dots (C-dots) feature a considerable step toward their potential use in chemical sensors and biotechnology. Herein, by coupling phosphorus and nitrogen element introduction, the adenosine-derived N/P co-doped C-dots with fluorescence enhancement were achieved. By separately employing adenosine, adenosine monophosphate, adenosine diphosphate, and adenosine-5′-triphosphate as precursors, the effect of N/P co-doping on the fluorescence emission is discussed in detail. The formed C-dots with adenosine monophosphate exhibited strong blue fluorescence with a high quantum yield of 33.81%. Then the C-dots were employed as a fluorescent probe and utilized to develop a fast, sensitive, and selective picric acid sensor. The fluorescence of C-dots can be quenched by picric acid immediately, giving rise to a picric acid determination down to 30 nM. The possible mechanism of fluorescence quenching was discussed, which was proved to be inner filter effect and static quenching. Moreover, this method has the potential to detect picric acid in environmental water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
N/P co-doping
Picric acid
Fluorescent sensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The synthesis and characterization of C-dots
PA detection using N/P co-doped C-dots prepared with AMP
Application of PA detection in environmental water samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustrations of the route for adenosine-derived doped carbon dots synthesis and the corresponding quantum yield.

(A) The photograph of the different C-dot solutions under visible (upper) and UV light (365 nm, lower). (B) The normalized fluorescence emission spectra of C-dot solutions produced from A (a), AMP (b), ADP (c), and ATP (d), respectively (excitation wavelength, 365 nm). The fluorescence excitation (dash dot line) and emission spectra (solid line) and UV–vis absorption spectrum of C-dots prepared with A (C), AMP (D), ADP (E), and ATP (F), respectively.
The TEM (A) and HRTEM (B) images of C-dots prepared with AMP. The XPS spectrum (C) and high-resolution C1s XPS spectrum (D) of C-dots prepared with AMP.
The fluorescence responses of N/P co-doped C-dots (dash dot line) and N doped C-dots (dashed line) for PA.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of C-dots in the presence of 0–100 μM PA. (B) The plot of fluorescence intensity ratio (F 0 − F)/F of C-dots with different concentrations of PA. The inset is the linear relationship between (F 0 − F)/F and the PA concentration from 0.1 to 30 μM. The fluorescence-quenched efficiency (ΔF/F 0) of C-dots with (C) the potential interferents and (D) the subsequent addition of 30 μM PA. Conditions: C-dots (4% (v/v)), pH 7.23.

(A) The absorption (Abs.), fluorescence excitation (Ex) and emission (Em) spectra of the N/P co-doped C-dots prepared with AMP. (B) Suppressed efficiency (E) of corrected (○) and observed (●) measurements for the C-dots after addition of different concentrations of PA. (C) The effect of different temperatures on the fluorescence intensity ratio ((F 0 − F)/F) with PA addition. (D) Decay curves of C-dots in the absence and presence of PA (30 μM). λex = 350 nm and λem = 421 nm. The data involved in Fig. 6B were taken from Table S6.

head.ce:title: Adenosine-derived doped carbon dots: From an insight into effect of N/P co-doping on emission to highly sensitive picric acid sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Eco-environments in Three Gorges Reservoir Region (Ministry of Education), School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Eco-environments in Three Gorges Reservoir Region (Ministry of Education)
School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The various synthetic routes of carbon dots (C-dots) feature a considerable step toward their potential use in chemical sensors and biotechnology. Herein, by coupling phosphorus and nitrogen element introduction, the adenosine-derived N/P co-doped C-dots with fluorescence enhancement were achieved. By separately employing adenosine, adenosine monophosphate, adenosine diphosphate, and adenosine-5′-triphosphate as precursors, the effect of N/P co-doping on the fluorescence emission is discussed in detail. The formed C-dots with adenosine monophosphate exhibited strong blue fluorescence with a high quantum yield of 33.81%. Then the C-dots were employed as a fluorescent probe and utilized to develop a fast, sensitive, and selective picric acid sensor. The fluorescence of C-dots can be quenched by picric acid immediately, giving rise to a picric acid determination down to 30 nM. The possible mechanism of fluorescence quenching was discussed, which was proved to be inner filter effect and static quenching. Moreover, this method has the potential to detect picric acid in environmental water samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, carbon nanodots (C-dots) arise as an attractive class of quasispherical nanocarbons with sizes below 10 nm [1–4]. Similar to the carbon-based materials such as graphene, the carbon nanotube, and fullerene, C-dots have attracted extensive interest because of their fascinating fluorescence properties, tunable surface functionalities, and well-defined shapes [5]. Since their serendipitous discovery by Scrivens et al. purifying single-walled carbon nanotubes [6], C-dots have been synthesized through a multitude of synthetic routes from molecules, polymers, and even carbogenic particles [7–9]. Most of them exhibit tunable fluorescence emission, resistance to photobleaching, good water solubility, and low toxicity. However, their quantum yields are relatively low (usually less than 30%). On the other hand, two major approaches of surface passivation (namely, surface functionalization, and doping with heteroatoms) have been reported to obtain fluorescence enhancement [10,11]. Surface functionalization arises as an effective pathway to achieve tailored properties of C-dots [12]. Meanwhile, by introducing heteroatoms (e.g., sulfur [13], phosphorus [14], nitrogen [15,16], etc.) into C-dots, doping becomes at the center of research effort due to the improved quantum yield of C-dots. Phosphorus, and nitrogen co-doped C-dots were synthesized by Gong et al. via hydrothermally heating the nucleotide adenosine-5′-triphosphate (ATP) [14]. Following a similar route and also using ATP as the nitrogen and phosphorus source, the N/P co-doped C-dots were prepared to develop a fluorescent nanoprobe for Fe3+ detection [17]. Unfortunately, there are only limited examples of N/P co-doped C-dots obtained from ATP. The effect of N/P co-doping on the fluorescence emission have not been discussed in detail.

Picric acid (PA) is a nitrated organic compound which is also named 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (TNP) by the IUPAC. Like other acidic phenols, PA is an explosive that is mainly used in munitions. Compared to 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT), PA exhibits high explosive velocity and brisance, which poses a potential safety threat. In the analytical field, PA has been widely used in the medicine, leather, and dye industries [18]. Over time, large amounts of PA in industrial wastewater are discharged to the environment. The buildup of PA in water gives rise to serious environment pollution and threatens the human health. The continuous ingestion of PA can bring damage to the eyes, skin, respiratory and digestive tract, and so on [19–22]. Therefore, it becomes increasingly important to develop reliable PA sensors in aqueous solution. To date, many techniques have been reported for PA detection, such as ionization mass-spectrometry (IMS) [23,24], X-ray dispersion [25], surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) [26], colorimetry [27,28], and fluorescent methods [29,30]. Among them, fluorescent methods have attracted much attention because of the simple operation, cost-effective instrumentation, and requisite sensitivity and selectivity. Many fluorescent PA sensors have been established with metal nanoclusters [31], small molecular fluorophores [32], semiconductor quantum dots [33], meta-organic frameworks [34], polymers [35,36], organic fluorescent nanoparticles [37] and so on. However, some of the sensors suffer from complex synthesis process, undesirable selectivity, and potential health and environmental risks. Thus, developing new eco-friendly fluorescent materials is still in great need for highly sensitive and selective PA determination.

In this work, a series of adenosine-derived doped C-dots were synthesized by employing adenosine (A), adenosine monophosphate (AMP), adenosine diphosphate (ADP), and ATP as nitrogen and phosphorus sources. Compared to the sole N-doped C-dots, the N/P co-doped C-dots exhibits fluorescence enhancement through the introduction of phosphorus element. Also, the phosphorus content acts as an important role in the fluorescence enhancement. By comparison, the C-dots produced from AMP exhibits the highest quantum yield. We demonstrate that rational phosphorus introduction can improve the quantum yield of the N-doped C-dots. The single-step synthetic procedure is simple, fast, and costless. The basic optical properties and morphology of C-dots have been investigated. Considering that PA can quench the fluorescence of the C-dots, a PA sensor was built to sensitively and selectively detect PA in aqueous solution. We reasoned the possible mechanism of PA-induced fluorescence quenching and it turned out to be a combined effect of inner filter effect (IFE) and static quenching between C-dots and PA.

Adenosine triphosphate (ATP), adenosine diphosphate (ADP), adenosine monophosphate (AMP), and adenosine (A) were obtained from Sangon Biotech. Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The nitroaromatics that include m-dinitrobenzene (m-DNB), 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT), p-nitrotoluene (p-NT), nitrobenzene (NB), 2,4-dinitrotoluene (2,4-DNT), and 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (picric acid, PA) were purchased from Tianjin Kermel Chemical Reagents Co., Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Other chemicals were of analytical reagent grade, and all the reagents involved in this assay were used as received. Ultrapure water with a resistivity of 18.2 MΩ cm was used throughout the experiment. Britton−Robinson (BR) buffer (40 mM, pH 3–11) was prepared based on the standard protocols.

Fluorescence measurements were performed by using a Hitachi F-4500 spectrofluorophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). UV–Vis absorption spectra were recorded with a UV–vis 2450 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) images were taken on a Tecnai G2 F20 operated at a voltage of 200 kV (FEI, USA). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was performed with an EscaLab-250 spectrometer equipped with an Al Kα source (Thermo, USA). Fluorescence lifetime decay measurements were performed on an FL920 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK).

For preparing the C-dots, A, AMP, ADP, or ATP (10 mM) was transferred in a 25 mL Teflon equipped stainless steel autoclave, respectively. After hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C for 8 h, the pale yellow C-dot solution was obtained. The C-dot solution was dialyzed against water through a 1000 MWCO dialysis film for 48 h to remove excess reagents. The resulting solution was collected and stored at 4 °C in a refrigerator before use.

To build this PA sensor, 50 μL of the C-dot solution was mixed with 400 μL of BR buffer (pH 7.23). Then 50 μL of PA with different concentrations was added to the above solution. After thorough shaking, the mixture was incubated at room temperature for 30 s. Finally, the fluorescence and UV–vis absorption spectra of the mixture were measured. For real sample analysis, sea water from the Yellow Sea (China), river water from the Jialing River (Chongqing, China), and tap water from the laboratory were employed. The removal of suspended impurities in these samples was performed with a 0.22 μm membrane. Thereafter, different concentrations of PA were added to the water samples. The following procedures were performed as that described above.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The synthesis and characterization of C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 A exhibits the synthetic route for adenosine-derived doped carbon dots. By separately employing A, AMP, ADP, and ATP as the ingredient, the doped C-dots were successfully synthesized after heating at 180 °C for 8 h. Interestingly, the quantum yield of these doped C-dots are quite different (Fig. 1B). Herein, the quantum yields of C-dots in water were determined relative to quinine sulfate, and the detailed method and corresponding data (Fig. S1 and Table S1) are shown in the Supplementary data. To clarify the quantum yield difference, the effect of phosphorus content in the molecules was explored and is shown in Table S1. The quantum yield of N/P co-doped C-dots shows a significant increasing trend compared to that of the sole N-doped C-dots. The N/P co-doped C-dots prepared with AMP show the highest quantum yield. With further increase of phosphorus content, the quantum yield of N/P co-doped C-dots (produced from ADP and ATP) is gradually decreased. The proper phosphorus content plays an important role in the enhancement of quantum yield of C-dots. Meanwhile, the synthetic conditions of C-dots prepared with AMP was investigated under different hydrothermal conditions. As shown in Table S2, the quantum yield was obtained as high as 33.81% under the optimized conditions (180 °C, 8 h), which is comparable to that of other carbon dots [13,38,39]. Therefore, C-dots prepared with AMP have the potential to be an excellent fluorescent nanoprobe because of their high quantum yield.

Thereafter, the optical properties and morphology of C-dots were characterized. As can be seen in Fig. 2 A, the C-dot solutions are pale yellow under visible light and show strong fluorescence under 365 nm UV light. Interestingly, C-dots prepared with A and ATP exhibit cyan fluorescence, whereas the fluorescence of C-dots prepared with AMP and ADP are blue. To shed light on the fluorescence difference, the fluorescence emission spectra were normalized and are shown in Fig. 2B. When the excitation wavelength is fixed at 365 nm, the emission spectra of C-dots prepared with A (curve a) and ATP (curve d) is red shifted compared to that of C-dots prepared with AMP (curve b) and ADP (curve c), giving rise to the obvious fluorescence change. Then, the absorption and fluorescence spectra of the synthesized C-dots were explored systematically. The maximum excitation and emission wavelengths of the C-dots were summarized and are shown in Table S1. Notably, the C-dots prepared with AMP (Fig. 2D) show no obvious shift in the emission spectra with changed excitation wavelengths, which is different from the other C-dots that exhibit excitation-dependent emission spectra (Fig. 2C, E, 2F). Especially, the emission peaks of C-dots produced from A and ATP greatly shifted with increasing excitation wavelengths. As a result, when exposed to the 365 UV light, the fluorescence of C-dots prepared with A and ATP shifts from blue to cyan.

The morphology of C-dots prepared with AMP was investigated and is shown in Fig. 3 A and B. The TEM image shows the discrete and quasi-spherical nanoparticles with an average size around 2.5 nm. The lattice spacing observed in the HRTEM image is 0.21 nm, consistent with the (100) facet of graphite [40]. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to confirm the elements on C-dots synthesized with AMP. The main composition turns out to be carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and phosphorus (Fig. 3C and Table S3). The high resolution C1s spectrum exhibits the peaks centered at 287.6, 286.1, 285.1, and 284.5 eV (Fig. 3D), which can be ascribed to C=O, C–N, C–P, and C–C, respectively [14]. In the high resolution N1s spectrum, the peaks at 400.2, 399.4, and 398.7 eV indicate the presence of N–H, pyrrolic N, and pyridinic N (Fig. S2A). The high resolution P2p spectrum reveals the presence of P=O (133.8 eV) and P–O (132.9 eV) (Fig. S2B) [41]. As a result, nitrogen and phosphorus co-doped C-dots were successfully synthesized by using AMP as carbon, nitrogen, and phosphorus sources.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PA detection using N/P co-doped C-dots prepared with AMP
ce:section.ce:para: The N/P co-doped C-dots prepared with AMP are highly fluorescent, water-soluble, which provide C-dots a potential route to chemical sensing. In this work, the fluorescence of C-dots was found gradually quenched with increasing PA concentrations, and thus a PA sensor can be developed. First, we investigated the fluorescence responses of N doped C-dots prepared with A and N/P co-doped C-dots prepared with AMP for PA detection. To facilitate the comparison between the C-dots, the fluorescence intensity of both C-dots is kept the same before the addition of PA. With increasing PA concentrations, the fluorescence of N/P co-doped C-dots is quenched more significantly than that of N doped C-dots (Fig. 4 ). It is inferred that PA can be bound to the surface of C-dots by the proton transfer mechanism, which results in the formation of a non-fluorescent ground-state complex [42,43]. Fig. S2B confirms the presence of phosphate groups on the surface of the N/P co-doped C-dots. The N/P co-doped C-dots become more electron-rich by introducing the phosphorus element. Thus, the PA-induced static quenching is facilitated, giving rise to the high efficient response of C-dots to PA. As the PA-induced significant fluorescence decrease of N/P co-doped C-dots is conducive to sensitive PA sensing, the N/P co-doped C-dots were employed to develop the PA sensor. Then, to optimize this PA detection method, we investigated the effect of C-dots concentration, reaction time, pH value, and ionic strength on the fluorescence-quenched efficiency (ΔF/F 0). Herein, ΔF = F − F 0, and F, F 0 respectively represent the fluorescence intensity of C-dots in the presence and absence of PA.

The concentration of C-dots was first optimized. As shown in Fig. S3A, the C-dots concentration shows negligible influence on the fluorescence-quenched efficiency. A concentration of 4% (v/v) C-dots was employed for PA detection. Considering that reaction time played an important role in the detection process, the response time of C-dots to PA was investigated. Fig. S3B exhibits that the quenched fluorescence by PA can be achieved in 30 s and keeps stable as time goes on. The fast and stable response of this sensor implies a convenient PA detection using C-dots without strict time constraints. To explore the effect of different pH values on this PA sensor, the fluorescence intensity of C-dots was tested with or without PA. As can be seen in Figs. S3C and S3D, the fluorescence intensity almost does not change, and the fluorescence-quenched efficiency keeps stable at different pH values. Thus, BR buffer (pH 7.23) was utilized in the following experiments. Finally, the influence of ionic strength was investigated and is shown in Figs. S3E and S3F. With increasing NaCl concentration, there is no obvious change in fluorescence intensity and fluorescence-quenched efficiency. This PA sensor remains stable even in 1 M NaCl solution and exhibits the potential application in the complicated system.

The PA sensing system was developed under optimal conditions. After the addition of PA with different concentrations, the corresponding fluorescence emission spectra of C-dots were recorded and are shown in Fig. 5 A. The fluorescence emission intensity is gradually decreased with increasing PA concentration, and the maximum emission wavelength does not shift obviously. The correlation between the fluorescence intensity ratio of C-dots and PA concentration was plotted (Fig. 5B). As was reported, the quenching of fluorescence can be described by the Stern−Volmer equation, F 0/F = 1 + K sv[Q]. In this equation, K sv is the Stern−Volmer quenching constant, [Q] is the concentration of quencher, and F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of quencher, respectively. The inset of Fig. 5B shows that the fluorescence intensity ratio (F 0 − F)/F (i.e., F 0/F − 1) is linearly dependent upon the PA concentration at lower levels (0.1–30 μM), where F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of the C-dots in the absence and presence of PA, respectively. The detection limit (LOD) was estimated to be 30 nM based on the 3 σ/slope rule. When compared to some other fluorescent PA sensors (e.g., small molecule, conjugated polymer, and C-dots), the developed sensor shows several prominent merits in both synthesis and detection process, such as facile preparation in aqueous medium, high quantum yield, and low detection limit (Tables S4 and S5).

With further increase of PA concentration, the Stern−Volmer plot deviates from linearity and increases exponentially. As previously reported [44–46], the presence of nonlinear Stern–Volmer plot is due to the fluorescence annihilation caused by static quenching or simultaneous dynamic and static quenching. To fit the nonlinear Stern–Volmer plot, an exponential equation was applied to the system,
(1)
F 0/F = a exp(V q[PA]) + b
where a, b, and V q are constants. By fitting the Stern–Volmer plot shown in Fig. 5B, the equation can be obtained as F 0/F = 2.760exp(0.014[PA]) – 1.870 with correlation coefficient (R) of 0.9998. When the PA concentration is very small, the quenching constant (K) was calculated to be 3.86 × 104 M−1 (K = aV q), suggesting the super quenching ability of PA towards C-dots.

To ensure that this PA sensor can be applied to real samples, the selectivity study was performed in this experiment. As shown in Fig. 5C, the PA-induced fluorescence-quenched efficiency is much higher than the latent interferents (p-NT, m-DNB, TNT, 2,4-DNT, NB, o-dihydroxybenzene (o-DHB), p-dihydroxybenzene (p-DHB), o-nitrophenol (o-NP), benzoic acid (BA), aniline (AN), phenol (CA), and methylbenzene (MB)). In addition, the fluorescence responses of C-dots to the above substances with PA added were also investigated for further confirming the good selectivity. Fig. 5D illustrates that the fluorescence-quenched efficiency of the potential interferents with PA is comparable to that of the sole addition of PA. These figures imply the good selectivity of the developed sensor, which shows the great potential of C-dots to complicated environment applications.

We deduced the possible mechanism of quenched fluorescence by PA based on the following investigation. As shown in Fig. 6 A, PA exhibits a broad absorption in 200–500 nm. The absorption spectrum of PA significantly overlaps with the extinction spectrum of C-dots, whereas no obvious overlap is seen between the absorption of other potential interferents and the extinction spectrum of C-dots (Fig. S4). Thus, the PA-induced fluorescence quenching can be attributed to the inner filter effect. As reported by Gauthie group [47–49], IFE can be corrected based on the absorption characteristics of the mixed solution of PA and C-dots and the cell geometry shown in Fig. S5 as described in the following equation:
(2)
F
cor
F
obsd
=
2.3 d
A
ex
1 −
10
− d
A
ex
10
g
A
cm
2 . 3 s
A
cm
1 −
10
− s
A
cm
where A em and A ex are the absorbance at the maximum emission wavelength (421 nm) and the excitation wavelength (350 nm), respectively. F obsd is the observed fluorescence intensity and F cor is the corrected fluorescence intensity that is obtained by removing IFE from F obsd. The parameters defined in Fig. S5 display the width of the cuvette (d = 1.00 cm), the distance between the edge of the excitation beam and the edge of the cuvette (g = 0.40 cm), and the thickness of excitation beam (s = 0.10 cm).

To explore the part of fluorescence quenching induced by IFE, the absorption spectra of C-dots with PA were recorded and are shown in Fig. S6. The corresponding data were calculated and are listed in Table S6. All these correction factors recorded in Table S6 do not exceed 1.8, which is well within the recommended range and underlines that the data are credible. After the removal of IFE from the observed fluorescence quenching, the observed and corrected fluorescence quenching efficiency is separately calculated and plotted versus the PA concentration (Fig. 6B). As a result, it turns out to be about 65% of the total suppressed efficiency due to IFE of PA to C-dots, which implies that IFE mainly dominates the fluorescence quenching. In addition, the fluorescence intensity ratios at different temperatures were tested and are exhibited in Fig. 6C. As the reaction temperature increases, the fluorescence intensity ratios induced by different concentrations of PA almost keep unchanged, which further demonstrates the IFE-induced fluorescence quenching.

Aside from the inner filter effect, we further investigated other possible mechanisms of the PA-induced fluorescence quenching. As shown in Fig. 6A, the emission spectrum of C-dots overlaps with the absorption spectrum of PA. Therefore, there is a possibility of resonance energy transfer (RET) from C-dots to PA. To further verify whether RET happens during the PA sensing process, the time-dependent fluorescence measurements of the C-dots were measured in the absence and presence of PA, respectively. As shown in Fig. 6D, the decay curve of C-dots overlaps with that of C-dots and PA mixture. The fluorescence lifetime of C-dots is 5.38 ns before the addition of PA. No obvious decrease of fluorescence lifetime is observed when PA is added (5.20 ns). According to the previous reports [35,44,50], the fluorescence lifetime of the fluorophore changes obviously in the presence of the target molecules during the RET process. Thus, we infer that RET is not responsible for the fluorescence quenching.

Generally speaking, fluorescence quenching usually results from static quenching effect (SQE) or dynamic quenching effect (DQE) or both of them. In the case of SQE, the nonfluorescent complex is formed between a ground-state fluorophore and the quencher, resulting in fluorescence quenching. In DQE, the excited-state fluorophore interacts with the quencher and can be nonradiatively deactivated upon collision. To determine whether the quenching mechanism is dynamic, static or both of them, the lifetimes of the C-dots were measured with and without PA, respectively. In the diffusion-controlled dynamic quenching, lifetime of the C-dots will become shorter. In the static quenching process, the C-dots bound to PA are in the non-emissive state and the unbound C-dots show their intrinsic lifetime. As shown in Fig. 6D, the lifetime of the C-dots remains almost unchanged with addition of 30 μM PA, indicating that the fluorescence quenching is through a static mechanism. In conclusion, IFE and SQE are considered as the main mechanisms for PA-induced fluorescence quenching process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of PA detection in environmental water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence signal can be affected due to the presence of potential interferents. Thus, it still remains a challenge for the application of fluorescent sensor in complicated real samples. To test whether the developed sensor can be applied to real samples, we took tap water, Jialing River water, and the Yellow Sea water samples as representatives. As shown in Table S7, PA is not found in these samples. After spiked with PA standard solution, the water samples show quenched fluorescence and the obtained PA concentrations are coincident with the spiked values. The recoveries of PA in the water samples are calculated to be 92.4–106.4%. The relative standard deviations (RSD) obtained from five repetitive assays range from 0.94 to 3.23%, indicating excellent repeatability of this fluorescent sensor. The satisfactory results of the recovery and RSD are benefited from the stability of the synthesized C-dots. The proposed fluorescent sensor shows great potential for PA detection in complicated water samples.

In this work, we reported that the introduction of nitrogen and phosphorus source greatly benefited the C-dots synthesis. The N/P co-doped C-dots were successfully prepared with A, AMP, ADP, and ATP as the molecular precursors, respectively. Compared to the C-dots separately produced from A, ADP, and ATP, the prepared C-dots with AMP exhibited strong fluorescence and a high quantum yield (33.81%). The mechanism of the PA-induced fluorescence quenching was discussed in detail, which turned out to be inner filter effect and static quenching. Importantly, the proposed fluorescent sensor can provide a sensitive and robust method for PA monitoring in environmental water samples.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21273174, 21675131), the Municipal Science Foundation of Chongqing City (No. CSTC-2015jcyjB50001), and the Innovation Foundation of Chongqing City for Postgraduate (CYB16053).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
Online data Online data

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2018.01.049
